instruction: Implement the hexagonal architecture pattern in TypeScript with ports and adapters, showing how to keep business logic independent of frameworks.
output:
<think>
Hexagonal architecture (ports and adapters) separates the application core from external concerns. The core defines ports (interfaces) and external systems implement adapters. In TypeScript, this maps naturally to interfaces and classes.

The key principle: the application core has no knowledge of HTTP, databases, or message queues. It only knows about its ports. This means the core is testable without any infrastructure.

Ports are of two types:
- Driving ports (primary): how the outside world calls in (e.g., use case interfaces)
- Driven ports (secondary): how the core calls out (e.g., repository interfaces)

Adapters:
- Driving adapters: HTTP controllers, CLI commands, event handlers
- Driven adapters: database repositories, API clients, message publishers

I'll implement a complete example with TypeScript interfaces for ports and separate adapter implementations.
</think>

```typescript
// ============================================================
// PORTS — Interfaces defined by the application core
// ============================================================

// Driven port: how the core persists data
export interface UserRepository {
  save(user: User): Promise<void>;
  findById(id: string): Promise<User | null>;
  findByEmail(email: string): Promise<User | null>;
  delete(id: string): Promise<void>;
}

// Driven port: how the core sends notifications
export interface NotificationPort {
  sendWelcomeEmail(email: string, name: string): Promise<void>;
  sendPasswordReset(email: string, token: string): Promise<void>;
}

// Driven port: how the core logs
export interface LoggerPort {
  info(message: string, context?: Record<string, unknown>): void;
  error(message: string, error?: Error, context?: Record<string, unknown>): void;
}

// ============================================================
// DOMAIN — Pure business logic, no framework imports
// ============================================================

export class User {
  constructor(
    public readonly id: string,
    public readonly email: string,
    public name: string,
    public status: 'active' | 'suspended' | 'deleted' = 'active',
    public readonly createdAt: Date = new Date(),
  ) {}

  static create(id: string, email: string, name: string): User {
    if (!email.includes('@')) throw new DomainError('Invalid email');
    if (name.length < 2) throw new DomainError('Name too short');
    return new User(id, email, name);
  }

  suspend(): void {
    if (this.status !== 'active') {
      throw new DomainError(`Cannot suspend user in ${this.status} state`);
    }
    this.status = 'suspended';
  }
}

export class DomainError extends Error {
  constructor(message: string) {
    super(message);
    this.name = 'DomainError';
  }
}

// ============================================================
// DRIVING PORT — Use case interface (how outside calls in)
// ============================================================

export interface CreateUserCommand {
  email: string;
  name: string;
}

export interface UserDTO {
  id: string;
  email: string;
  name: string;
  status: string;
}

// Driving port: the use case the outside world can invoke
export interface CreateUserUseCase {
  execute(command: CreateUserCommand): Promise<UserDTO>;
}

export interface GetUserUseCase {
  execute(id: string): Promise<UserDTO | null>;
}

// ============================================================
// APPLICATION CORE — Implements driving ports, uses driven ports
// ============================================================

export class CreateUserService implements CreateUserUseCase {
  constructor(
    private readonly userRepo: UserRepository,
    private readonly notifications: NotificationPort,
    private readonly logger: LoggerPort,
    private readonly idGenerator: () => string,
  ) {}

  async execute(command: CreateUserCommand): Promise<UserDTO> {
    // Check uniqueness
    const existing = await this.userRepo.findByEmail(command.email);
    if (existing) throw new DomainError('Email already registered');

    // Create domain entity
    const user = User.create(this.idGenerator(), command.email, command.name);

    // Persist via driven port
    await this.userRepo.save(user);
    this.logger.info('User created', { userId: user.id });

    // Notify via driven port
    await this.notifications.sendWelcomeEmail(user.email, user.name)
      .catch(err => this.logger.error('Welcome email failed', err));

    return this.toDTO(user);
  }

  private toDTO(user: User): UserDTO {
    return { id: user.id, email: user.email, name: user.name, status: user.status };
  }
}

// ============================================================
// DRIVEN ADAPTERS — Implement driven ports
// ============================================================

// In-memory adapter (testing)
export class InMemoryUserRepository implements UserRepository {
  private store = new Map<string, User>();

  async save(user: User): Promise<void> { this.store.set(user.id, user); }
  async findById(id: string): Promise<User | null> { return this.store.get(id) ?? null; }
  async findByEmail(email: string): Promise<User | null> {
    return [...this.store.values()].find(u => u.email === email) ?? null;
  }
  async delete(id: string): Promise<void> { this.store.delete(id); }
}

// Console notification adapter (development)
export class ConsoleNotificationAdapter implements NotificationPort {
  async sendWelcomeEmail(email: string, name: string): Promise<void> {
    console.log(`[EMAIL] Welcome ${name} (${email})`);
  }
  async sendPasswordReset(email: string, token: string): Promise<void> {
    console.log(`[EMAIL] Password reset for ${email}: ${token}`);
  }
}

// ============================================================
// DRIVING ADAPTER — HTTP controller (Express/Fastify)
// ============================================================

// This adapter translates HTTP requests to use case calls
export function createUserHandler(useCase: CreateUserUseCase) {
  return async (req: { body: { email: string; name: string } }, res: any) => {
    try {
      const result = await useCase.execute(req.body);
      res.status(201).json(result);
    } catch (err) {
      if (err instanceof DomainError) {
        res.status(400).json({ error: err.message });
      } else {
        res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal error' });
      }
    }
  };
}

// ============================================================
// COMPOSITION ROOT — Wire everything together
// ============================================================

function bootstrap() {
  const repo = new InMemoryUserRepository();
  const notifications = new ConsoleNotificationAdapter();
  const logger: LoggerPort = { info: console.log, error: console.error };
  const idGen = () => `usr_${Date.now()}`;

  const createUser = new CreateUserService(repo, notifications, logger, idGen);
  // Swap to PostgresUserRepository in production — zero changes to core

  return { createUser };
}
```

The hexagonal architecture keeps the application core (CreateUserService, User entity) completely isolated from frameworks. The core defines driven ports (UserRepository, NotificationPort) as interfaces and driving ports (CreateUserUseCase) as the API contract. Adapters implement the interfaces — swap InMemoryUserRepository for PostgresUserRepository by changing one line in the composition root. The HTTP handler is a thin driving adapter that translates HTTP to use case calls.

instruction: Implement an event-driven frontend architecture in TypeScript using an event bus with typed events, state management, and component communication.
output:
<think>
Event-driven architecture in the frontend decouples components by communicating through events instead of direct method calls. This is especially valuable in complex SPAs where component trees are deep and cross-cutting concerns are common.

Key design decisions:
1. Type safety — events should be typed so subscribers know exactly what payload they'll receive.
2. Event bus — centralized pub/sub. Must support subscribe, unsubscribe, and wildcard patterns.
3. State management — events can drive state changes. The event bus becomes the source of truth for what happened.
4. Component lifecycle — subscriptions must be cleaned up when components unmount.
5. Middleware — ability to intercept events for logging, validation, or transformation.

TypeScript's type system is perfect for this: we can define an event map that maps event names to payload types, then use generics to enforce type safety at compile time.
</think>

```typescript
// ============================================================
// Typed Event System with compile-time safety
// ============================================================

// Define all events and their payloads in one place
interface EventMap {
  'user:login': { userId: string; email: string };
  'user:logout': { userId: string };
  'cart:add': { productId: string; quantity: number };
  'cart:remove': { productId: string };
  'cart:checkout': { orderId: string; total: number };
  'notification:show': { message: string; type: 'info' | 'error' | 'success' };
  'theme:change': { theme: 'light' | 'dark' };
}

type EventName = keyof EventMap;
type EventHandler<E extends EventName> = (payload: EventMap[E]) => void;
type Unsubscribe = () => void;

// Middleware receives event name, payload, and a next function
type Middleware = (
  event: EventName,
  payload: unknown,
  next: () => void
) => void;

class TypedEventBus {
  private handlers = new Map<EventName, Set<EventHandler<any>>>();
  private middlewares: Middleware[] = [];
  private history: Array<{ event: EventName; payload: unknown; timestamp: number }> = [];
  private maxHistory = 100;

  on<E extends EventName>(event: E, handler: EventHandler<E>): Unsubscribe {
    if (!this.handlers.has(event)) {
      this.handlers.set(event, new Set());
    }
    this.handlers.get(event)!.add(handler);

    // Return unsubscribe function for cleanup
    return () => {
      this.handlers.get(event)?.delete(handler);
    };
  }

  once<E extends EventName>(event: E, handler: EventHandler<E>): Unsubscribe {
    const wrappedHandler: EventHandler<E> = (payload) => {
      handler(payload);
      unsub();
    };
    const unsub = this.on(event, wrappedHandler);
    return unsub;
  }

  emit<E extends EventName>(event: E, payload: EventMap[E]): void {
    // Record in history
    this.history.push({ event, payload, timestamp: Date.now() });
    if (this.history.length > this.maxHistory) this.history.shift();

    // Run middleware chain
    let index = 0;
    const next = () => {
      if (index < this.middlewares.length) {
        this.middlewares[index++](event, payload, next);
      } else {
        // Deliver to handlers
        this.handlers.get(event)?.forEach(handler => {
          try { handler(payload); }
          catch (err) { console.error(`Event handler error [${event}]:`, err); }
        });
      }
    };
    next();
  }

  use(middleware: Middleware): void {
    this.middlewares.push(middleware);
  }

  getHistory(): ReadonlyArray<{ event: EventName; payload: unknown; timestamp: number }> {
    return this.history;
  }

  clear(): void {
    this.handlers.clear();
    this.history = [];
  }
}

// ============================================================
// State Management driven by events
// ============================================================

interface AppState {
  user: { id: string; email: string } | null;
  cart: Array<{ productId: string; quantity: number }>;
  theme: 'light' | 'dark';
  notifications: Array<{ id: number; message: string; type: string }>;
}

class StateStore {
  private state: AppState = {
    user: null,
    cart: [],
    theme: 'light',
    notifications: [],
  };
  private listeners = new Set<(state: AppState) => void>();
  private notificationCounter = 0;

  constructor(private bus: TypedEventBus) {
    // Events drive state changes
    bus.on('user:login', ({ userId, email }) => {
      this.update({ user: { id: userId, email } });
    });

    bus.on('user:logout', () => {
      this.update({ user: null, cart: [] });
    });

    bus.on('cart:add', ({ productId, quantity }) => {
      const existing = this.state.cart.find(i => i.productId === productId);
      if (existing) {
        existing.quantity += quantity;
        this.update({ cart: [...this.state.cart] });
      } else {
        this.update({ cart: [...this.state.cart, { productId, quantity }] });
      }
    });

    bus.on('cart:remove', ({ productId }) => {
      this.update({ cart: this.state.cart.filter(i => i.productId !== productId) });
    });

    bus.on('theme:change', ({ theme }) => {
      this.update({ theme });
    });

    bus.on('notification:show', ({ message, type }) => {
      const id = ++this.notificationCounter;
      this.update({
        notifications: [...this.state.notifications, { id, message, type }],
      });
      // Auto-dismiss after 5 seconds
      setTimeout(() => {
        this.update({
          notifications: this.state.notifications.filter(n => n.id !== id),
        });
      }, 5000);
    });
  }

  getState(): Readonly<AppState> {
    return this.state;
  }

  subscribe(listener: (state: AppState) => void): Unsubscribe {
    this.listeners.add(listener);
    return () => this.listeners.delete(listener);
  }

  private update(partial: Partial<AppState>): void {
    this.state = { ...this.state, ...partial };
    this.listeners.forEach(l => l(this.state));
  }
}

// ============================================================
// Component base with auto-cleanup
// ============================================================

abstract class EventDrivenComponent {
  private unsubscribers: Unsubscribe[] = [];

  constructor(protected bus: TypedEventBus) {}

  protected listen<E extends EventName>(event: E, handler: EventHandler<E>): void {
    this.unsubscribers.push(this.bus.on(event, handler));
  }

  destroy(): void {
    this.unsubscribers.forEach(unsub => unsub());
    this.unsubscribers = [];
  }
}

// Example component
class CartWidget extends EventDrivenComponent {
  constructor(bus: TypedEventBus) {
    super(bus);
    this.listen('cart:add', ({ productId, quantity }) => {
      this.render(`Added ${quantity}x ${productId}`);
    });
    this.listen('cart:remove', ({ productId }) => {
      this.render(`Removed ${productId}`);
    });
  }

  private render(msg: string): void {
    console.log(`[CartWidget] ${msg}`);
  }
}

// ============================================================
// Usage with middleware
// ============================================================

const bus = new TypedEventBus();

// Logging middleware
bus.use((event, payload, next) => {
  console.log(`[Event] ${event}`, payload);
  next();
});

const store = new StateStore(bus);
const cart = new CartWidget(bus);

// Type-safe: TypeScript enforces correct payload shape
bus.emit('user:login', { userId: 'u1', email: 'dev@test.com' });
bus.emit('cart:add', { productId: 'widget-a', quantity: 2 });
// bus.emit('cart:add', { wrong: 'field' });  // Compile error!

cart.destroy(); // Cleans up all subscriptions
```

The EventMap interface provides compile-time safety — emitting 'cart:add' with wrong payload fields is a type error. Components extend EventDrivenComponent for automatic subscription cleanup on destroy. The StateStore reacts to events to update application state, and middleware provides cross-cutting concerns (logging, analytics, validation) without modifying event handlers.

instruction: Build a dependency injection container in TypeScript that supports constructor injection with decorators, singleton/transient scopes, and circular dependency detection.
output:
<think>
TypeScript DI containers typically use decorators and reflect-metadata to inspect constructor parameter types. This allows automatic resolution of dependencies based on type information.

Key design decisions:
1. Registration — bind interfaces to implementations using tokens (symbols or strings)
2. Scopes — singleton (one instance) vs transient (new instance each time)
3. Auto-wiring — use decorator metadata to resolve constructor parameters
4. Circular dependency detection — track resolution stack
5. Lazy resolution — use proxies or factories to break circular deps

I'll implement this with TypeScript decorators (stage 3/legacy) and reflect-metadata for parameter type inspection. The container will support both explicit registration and decorator-based auto-registration.
</think>

```typescript
import 'reflect-metadata';

// ============================================================
// Container types
// ============================================================

type Constructor<T = any> = new (...args: any[]) => T;
type Token<T = any> = Constructor<T> | symbol | string;

enum Scope {
  Singleton = 'singleton',
  Transient = 'transient',
}

interface Registration<T = any> {
  token: Token<T>;
  implementation: Constructor<T>;
  scope: Scope;
  instance?: T; // Cached singleton
  factory?: (container: Container) => T;
}

// ============================================================
// Decorators
// ============================================================

const INJECTABLE_KEY = Symbol('injectable');
const INJECT_KEY = Symbol('inject');

// Mark a class as injectable
function Injectable(scope: Scope = Scope.Transient): ClassDecorator {
  return (target: Function) => {
    Reflect.defineMetadata(INJECTABLE_KEY, { scope }, target);
  };
}

// Override parameter injection token
function Inject(token: Token): ParameterDecorator {
  return (target, _propertyKey, paramIndex) => {
    const existing = Reflect.getMetadata(INJECT_KEY, target) || {};
    existing[paramIndex] = token;
    Reflect.defineMetadata(INJECT_KEY, existing, target);
  };
}

// ============================================================
// Container
// ============================================================

class ContainerError extends Error {
  constructor(message: string) {
    super(message);
    this.name = 'ContainerError';
  }
}

class Container {
  private registrations = new Map<Token, Registration>();
  private resolvingStack = new Set<Token>(); // Circular dep detection

  register<T>(token: Token<T>, implementation: Constructor<T>, scope?: Scope): this {
    const meta = Reflect.getMetadata(INJECTABLE_KEY, implementation);
    this.registrations.set(token, {
      token,
      implementation,
      scope: scope ?? meta?.scope ?? Scope.Transient,
    });
    return this;
  }

  registerSingleton<T>(token: Token<T>, implementation: Constructor<T>): this {
    return this.register(token, implementation, Scope.Singleton);
  }

  registerInstance<T>(token: Token<T>, instance: T): this {
    this.registrations.set(token, {
      token,
      implementation: Object.getPrototypeOf(instance).constructor,
      scope: Scope.Singleton,
      instance,
    });
    return this;
  }

  registerFactory<T>(token: Token<T>, factory: (c: Container) => T, scope?: Scope): this {
    this.registrations.set(token, {
      token,
      implementation: class {} as any,
      scope: scope ?? Scope.Transient,
      factory,
    });
    return this;
  }

  resolve<T>(token: Token<T>): T {
    const reg = this.registrations.get(token);
    if (!reg) {
      // Try auto-registration for constructor tokens
      if (typeof token === 'function') {
        const meta = Reflect.getMetadata(INJECTABLE_KEY, token);
        if (meta) {
          this.register(token, token as Constructor<T>, meta.scope);
          return this.resolve(token);
        }
      }
      throw new ContainerError(
        `No registration for ${String(token)}. Did you forget @Injectable()?`
      );
    }

    // Return cached singleton
    if (reg.scope === Scope.Singleton && reg.instance !== undefined) {
      return reg.instance as T;
    }

    // Circular dependency detection
    if (this.resolvingStack.has(token)) {
      const chain = [...this.resolvingStack].map(String).join(' -> ');
      throw new ContainerError(`Circular dependency: ${chain} -> ${String(token)}`);
    }

    this.resolvingStack.add(token);
    try {
      let instance: T;
      if (reg.factory) {
        instance = reg.factory(this);
      } else {
        instance = this.createInstance(reg.implementation);
      }

      if (reg.scope === Scope.Singleton) {
        reg.instance = instance;
      }
      return instance;
    } finally {
      this.resolvingStack.delete(token);
    }
  }

  private createInstance<T>(ctor: Constructor<T>): T {
    // Get constructor parameter types via reflect-metadata
    const paramTypes: Constructor[] = Reflect.getMetadata('design:paramtypes', ctor) || [];
    const injectOverrides: Record<number, Token> = Reflect.getMetadata(INJECT_KEY, ctor) || {};

    const deps = paramTypes.map((type, index) => {
      const token = injectOverrides[index] || type;
      return this.resolve(token);
    });

    return new ctor(...deps);
  }

  // Validate all registrations can be resolved
  validate(): string[] {
    const errors: string[] = [];
    for (const [token] of this.registrations) {
      try {
        this.resolve(token);
      } catch (e) {
        errors.push((e as Error).message);
      }
    }
    return errors;
  }
}

// ============================================================
// Usage
// ============================================================

// Define interfaces via abstract classes (for reflect-metadata)
abstract class Logger {
  abstract log(msg: string): void;
}

abstract class UserRepo {
  abstract findUser(id: string): { id: string; name: string } | null;
}

@Injectable(Scope.Singleton)
class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
  log(msg: string): void { console.log(`[LOG] ${msg}`); }
}

@Injectable(Scope.Transient)
class InMemoryUserRepo extends UserRepo {
  constructor(private logger: Logger) { super(); }
  findUser(id: string) {
    this.logger.log(`Finding user ${id}`);
    return { id, name: 'Test User' };
  }
}

@Injectable()
class UserService {
  constructor(
    private repo: UserRepo,
    private logger: Logger,
  ) {}

  getUser(id: string) {
    this.logger.log(`Getting user ${id}`);
    return this.repo.findUser(id);
  }
}

// Wire up
const container = new Container();
container.registerSingleton(Logger, ConsoleLogger);
container.register(UserRepo, InMemoryUserRepo);

const service = container.resolve(UserService); // Auto-wired!
service.getUser('123');
```

The container uses reflect-metadata to inspect constructor parameter types at runtime, automatically resolving dependencies. Singleton scope caches instances, transient creates new ones each resolution. Circular dependencies are detected via a resolution stack — if a token appears while it's already being resolved, the container throws with the full dependency chain. The @Inject decorator overrides type-based resolution with explicit tokens for interface bindings.

instruction: Implement a module pattern in TypeScript for organizing a large application with barrel exports, lazy loading, and clear dependency boundaries between features.
output:
<think>
Large TypeScript applications need careful module organization to maintain build times, code clarity, and separation of concerns. Key patterns:

1. Feature modules — group related code by feature, not by type
2. Barrel exports (index.ts) — re-export public API from a single entry point
3. Dependency boundaries — features should not import internal modules from other features
4. Lazy loading — dynamically import feature modules for code splitting
5. Shared modules — common utilities that multiple features depend on

The module boundary rule is critical: Feature A can import Feature B's public API (barrel export) but not its internal modules. This prevents tight coupling and enables independent refactoring.

I'll implement a module system with enforced boundaries, lazy loading support, and clear public/private distinctions.
</think>

```typescript
// ============================================================
// Project structure:
//   src/
//     shared/           -- Shared utilities (no feature deps)
//       index.ts        -- Barrel export
//       types.ts
//       utils.ts
//     features/
//       auth/           -- Auth feature module
//         index.ts      -- PUBLIC API (barrel export)
//         internal/     -- PRIVATE (never import from outside)
//           auth.service.ts
//           auth.store.ts
//         types.ts
//       products/       -- Products feature module
//         index.ts
//         internal/
//           product.service.ts
//           product.store.ts
//         types.ts
//     app.ts            -- Composition root
// ============================================================

// --- shared/types.ts ---
export interface Result<T> {
  success: boolean;
  data?: T;
  error?: string;
}

export interface Disposable {
  dispose(): void;
}

// --- shared/utils.ts ---
export function generateId(): string {
  return `${Date.now()}-${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2)}`;
}

export function debounce<T extends (...args: any[]) => void>(fn: T, ms: number): T {
  let timer: ReturnType<typeof setTimeout>;
  return ((...args: any[]) => {
    clearTimeout(timer);
    timer = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), ms);
  }) as T;
}

// --- shared/index.ts (barrel export) ---
export type { Result, Disposable } from './types';
export { generateId, debounce } from './utils';

// ============================================================
// Feature Module: Auth
// ============================================================

// --- features/auth/types.ts ---
export interface AuthUser {
  id: string;
  email: string;
  name: string;
  token: string;
}

export interface LoginCredentials {
  email: string;
  password: string;
}

// --- features/auth/internal/auth.store.ts ---
// PRIVATE — only imported within auth feature
import type { AuthUser } from '../types';

type AuthListener = (user: AuthUser | null) => void;

export class AuthStore {
  private currentUser: AuthUser | null = null;
  private listeners = new Set<AuthListener>();

  getUser(): AuthUser | null { return this.currentUser; }
  isAuthenticated(): boolean { return this.currentUser !== null; }

  setUser(user: AuthUser | null): void {
    this.currentUser = user;
    this.listeners.forEach(l => l(user));
  }

  subscribe(listener: AuthListener): () => void {
    this.listeners.add(listener);
    return () => this.listeners.delete(listener);
  }
}

// --- features/auth/internal/auth.service.ts ---
import type { LoginCredentials, AuthUser } from '../types';
import { AuthStore } from './auth.store';
import type { Result } from '../../../shared';

export class AuthService {
  constructor(private store: AuthStore) {}

  async login(creds: LoginCredentials): Promise<Result<AuthUser>> {
    try {
      // API call would go here
      const user: AuthUser = {
        id: 'u1', email: creds.email,
        name: 'User', token: 'jwt-token',
      };
      this.store.setUser(user);
      return { success: true, data: user };
    } catch (err) {
      return { success: false, error: String(err) };
    }
  }

  logout(): void {
    this.store.setUser(null);
  }
}

// --- features/auth/index.ts (PUBLIC API) ---
// Only export what other features are allowed to use
import { AuthStore } from './internal/auth.store';
import { AuthService } from './internal/auth.service';

export type { AuthUser, LoginCredentials } from './types';

// Factory function — hides internal wiring
export function createAuthModule() {
  const store = new AuthStore();
  const service = new AuthService(store);

  return {
    // Public methods
    login: service.login.bind(service),
    logout: service.logout.bind(service),
    getUser: store.getUser.bind(store),
    isAuthenticated: store.isAuthenticated.bind(store),
    onAuthChange: store.subscribe.bind(store),
  };
}

export type AuthModule = ReturnType<typeof createAuthModule>;

// ============================================================
// Lazy Loading — dynamic import for code splitting
// ============================================================

type LazyModule<T> = {
  load(): Promise<T>;
  isLoaded(): boolean;
  get(): T | null;
};

function createLazyModule<T>(loader: () => Promise<T>): LazyModule<T> {
  let instance: T | null = null;
  let loading: Promise<T> | null = null;

  return {
    async load(): Promise<T> {
      if (instance) return instance;
      if (loading) return loading;
      loading = loader().then(mod => {
        instance = mod;
        return mod;
      });
      return loading;
    },
    isLoaded: () => instance !== null,
    get: () => instance,
  };
}

// --- app.ts (composition root) ---
const authModule = createLazyModule(async () => {
  const { createAuthModule } = await import('./features/auth');
  return createAuthModule();
});

// Products loads only when needed
const productsModule = createLazyModule(async () => {
  const { createProductsModule } = await import('./features/products');
  const auth = await authModule.load(); // Ensure auth is ready
  return createProductsModule(auth);
});

// Usage:
async function main() {
  const auth = await authModule.load();
  await auth.login({ email: 'dev@test.com', password: 'secret' });

  // Products module loads on demand
  if (auth.isAuthenticated()) {
    const products = await productsModule.load();
    // products.listProducts();
  }
}
```

Feature modules expose only their public API through barrel exports (index.ts). Internal modules are in an `internal/` directory and should never be imported from outside the feature. The `createAuthModule` factory hides internal wiring (AuthStore, AuthService are internal implementation details). Lazy loading via dynamic `import()` enables code splitting — the products feature only loads when the user is authenticated. The `LazyModule` wrapper provides safe access patterns (load once, cache, check readiness).
